---
title: "Explore DaSEH one year post survey quantitative responses"
author: "Sarah Teichman"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: html_document
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
library(surveydown)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggstats)
library(ggalluvial)
```

First, load and clean data (filter on consent, check IP addresses and manually remove or merge duplicates, remove responses that are mostly `NA` or test).

```{r, echo = FALSE, eval = TRUE, results = 'hide', message = FALSE, warning = FALSE}
db <- sd_db_connect(gssencmode = "disable")
dat <- sd_get_data(db) %>% filter(consent == "yes")
```

```{r, echo = FALSE, eval = TRUE, results = 'hide', message = FALSE, warning = FALSE}
length(unique(dat$ip_address)) # 27, 4 repeats
table(as.numeric(as.factor(dat$ip_address)))
which(as.numeric(as.factor(dat$ip_address)) == 2)
which(as.numeric(as.factor(dat$ip_address)) == 11)
which(as.numeric(as.factor(dat$ip_address)) == 24)

dat[c(20, 23, 24), ] # keep 20, drop 23 and 24 which are mostly NA
dat[c(4, 13), ] # keep 4, drop 13 which is mostly NA
dat[c(16, 19), ] # the same person, made it partway through in 16, then finished in 19, need to manually combine some text
dat$coding_practices[19] <- dat$coding_practices[16]
dat$reproducibility_practices[19] <- dat$reproducibility_practices[16]
dat$part_of_journey_text[19] <- paste0(dat$part_of_journey_text[16], dat$part_of_journey_text[19])

dat <- dat[-c(13, 16, 23, 24), ]

"test" %in% as.matrix(dat) # check for "test" entries

# check for mostly NA entries:
dat %>% 
  select(-contains("time"), -why_not_using_r) %>%
  mutate(num_na = rowSums(is.na(across(everything())))) %>%
  arrange(desc(num_na)) %>%
  head(6)
# the remaining entries with the most NAs are people who skipped the free responses, so keep these 
# respondents in the analysis 
```

We are left with  `r nrow(dat)` responses. 

### How often did you use R before DaSEH? Now? 

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.height = 4}
using_R_likert <- dat %>%
  mutate(across(c(how_often_before, how_often_now), 
                ~factor(.x, levels = c("never", "few", "occasionally",
                                       "weekly", "extensively"))))
using_R_likert %>%
  select(how_often_before, how_often_now) %>%
  gglikert(variable_labels = c(
    how_often_before = "Frequency of using R before DaSEH",
    how_often_now = "Frequency of using R now"
    ),
  labels_size = 2, y_label_wrap = 35) + 
  theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size = 7))
```

Here we see that before DaSEH this group of participants was quite heterogeneous with how often they used R, with the largest groups of participants who never used R (22%) or occasionally used R (33%). Now, 88% of participants use R occasionally or more often, and the largest groups of participants use R occasionally (44%) or extensively (37%). 

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.height = 4, warning = FALSE}
likert_levels <- c("never", "few", "occasionally", "weekly", "extensively")
likert_levels_before <- paste0(likert_levels, " (",
                               using_R_likert %>% count(how_often_before) %>% pull(n),
                               ")")
likert_levels_now <- paste0(likert_levels, " (",
                            using_R_likert %>% count(how_often_now) %>% pull(n) %>% c(0, .),
                            ")")

counts <- using_R_likert %>%
  count(how_often_before, how_often_now, name = "n") %>%
  mutate(
    y_before = as.numeric(how_often_before),
    y_now    = as.numeric(how_often_now)
  )

counts %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = 0, xend = 1, y = y_before, yend = y_now)) +
  geom_segment(aes(linewidth = n, color = as.factor(y_before)), alpha = 0.7, lineend = "round") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = c(0, 1), 
    labels = c("Frequency of using R\nbefore DaSEH", "Frequency of using R now")) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq_along(likert_levels), labels = likert_levels_before,
    expand = expansion(add = 0.35),
    sec.axis = dup_axis(
      name   = NULL,
      labels = likert_levels_now,
      breaks = seq_along(likert_levels)
    )
  ) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  ) + 
  guides(color = "none", linewidth = "none")
```

From this plot, we can see that the participants with the least R experience ("never" and "few") all moved to using R more often (most to "occasionally"), and the participants with the most R experience before ("weekly" and "extensively") nearly all are "extensive" users now. The most variation in outcomes is from the participants who started off using R occasionally. 

Interestingly, there are three participants who use R less frequently now compared to before DaSEH. For the two participants that went from using R occasionally to few, this is explained in their free response answers to later questions (one person started a new position that doesn't usually use R but said they look forward to using R in the future, and the second person wanted to learn R to instruct a data science course but the course is using different tools). The participant who went from a weekly user to an occasional user does not have a free response that explains this change. 

```{r, include = FALSE}
dat %>% 
  filter((how_often_before == "occasionally" & how_often_now == "few") |
           (how_often_before == "weekly" & how_often_now == "occasionally")) %>%
  select(contains("often"), contains("text"), -contains("time"))
```

### Did you participate in the code-a-thon at the Hutch?

```{r, echo = FALSE}
dat %>% count(codeathon)
```

### What do you use R for?

```{r, echo = FALSE}
r_use <- dat %>%
  select(r_use_cases) %>%
  separate_longer_delim(r_use_cases, delim = ",") %>%
  mutate(r_use_cases = str_trim(r_use_cases) %>%
           str_replace_all("_", " "))

r_use %>%
  filter(!is.na(r_use_cases)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = fct_infreq(r_use_cases))) + 
  geom_bar() + 
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 1, vjust = 0.5)) +
  labs(x = "R use cases", y = "Count")
```

```{r, include = FALSE}
r_use %>% count(r_use_cases) %>% arrange(desc(n))
```

Data cleaning is the most common answer (all 24 participants who responded to this question included data cleaning), followed by visualization (23) and data analysis (22). The impact of learning data cleaning comes up in the free responses below as well. 

### Why aren't you using R? 

```{r, echo = FALSE}
dat %>%
  count(why_not_using_r)
```

### Have you continued learning R through other courses and materials?

```{r, echo = FALSE}
dat %>% count(courses_materials)
```

### Have you recommended DaSEH materials to others?

```{r, echo = FALSE}
dat %>% count(recommended)
```

### Have you returned to any DaSEH materials?

```{r, echo = FALSE}
dat %>%
  select(returned) %>%
  separate_longer_delim(returned, delim = ",") %>%
  mutate(returned = str_trim(returned)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = fct_infreq(returned))) + 
  geom_bar() + 
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 1, vjust = 0.5)) +
  labs(x = "Materials returned to")
```

Out of the 27 respondents, 20 returned to slides, 19 returned to cheatsheets, 4 returned to videos, and 4 returned to no materials. 

### DaSEH was an important part of my programming journey.

```{r fig.height = 4, echo = FALSE}
journey_likert <- dat %>%
  mutate(part_of_journey = str_replace_all(part_of_journey, "_", " ")) %>%
  mutate(across(part_of_journey, ~factor(.x, levels = c("strong disagree", "disagree", "neutral",
                                                     "agree", "strong agree")))) %>%
  tibble::as_tibble()
journey_likert %>%
  select(part_of_journey) %>%
  gglikert(variable_labels = c(
    part_of_journey = "DaSEH was an important part of my programming journey"
    ),
  labels_size = 2, y_label_wrap = 35) + 
  theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size = 7))
```

There were 4 respondents who said "strongly disagree". These are a subset of their responses to the other questions. 

```{r, echo = FALSE}
disagree <- dat %>%
  filter(part_of_journey == "strong_disagree")
disagree %>%
  select(how_often_before, how_often_now, codeathon, r_use_cases, returned, why_not_using_r)
```
